body_txt: If you need a recreational or medical substance for a storyline, there's always one old standby: make one up. This meshes perfectly with Speculative Fiction , but would seem completely out of place in a realistic series. After all, the planet "Yalic 4" might not have the same plants or minerals etc. that a fictional analogue of Earth would. Alternatively, if it's a comedy, you could get away with I Can't Believe It's Not Heroin! instead. Otherwise you can resort to the potentially narmy G-Rated Drug . Aside from a writer's hesitancy to show a beloved character using drugs, Media Watchdogs often cracked down on any depiction of drugs (even if they were negative) for many years. Lately it's been reduced to "heavily sanctioned" at best, creating the unfortunate irony that incorrect portrayal of the effect of drugs has made audiences more liable to dismiss the true effects of drugs as propaganda. In Real Life , these are known as "designer drugs", for people who want to get high without using technically illegal street drugs. Often can be the Spice of Life . Compare Super Serum , Undiscriminating Addict . Not to be confused (usually) with Psycho Serum . Addictive Magic is closely related, as is A.I. Getting High . Compare Alien Catnip when a mundane substance proves a drug to aliens. May be the cure for a Fictional Disease . It can go with Drug-Crazed Savagery , which is when characters become aggressive and perform dangerous activities under the influence of drugs and alcohol. Despite the common useage of "drug" meaning an addictive, possibly psychedelic recreational substance, many drugs are medicinal or utilitarian (and there is some overlap). The active agent of Uncoffee , if it isn't caffeine, may be one of these. A Chemical Messiah , any fantastical version of an Anti-Radiation Drug , any kind of Super or Truth serum, etc, is going to be a fictional drug in almost any setting where chemicals are known and identified. Often, Side Effects Include... something entirely fantastical. Goes with Drug Based Characterization , where a character's needs and motivations are reflected by the drugs they take.
